question: why are farmers market
title: Why Farmers Markets Matter More Than You Think
metaDescription: Farmers markets aren't just about fresh produce. They're where your local food community lives and breathes. Here's why showing up matters — for you, your neighbors, and the farmers who feed y'all.
content:
Hey everybody. I was walking around the Urban Harvest Farmers Market on a Saturday morning in Houston, watching people stop and talk to farmers, taste samples, let their kids pet a neighbor's dog, and tuck vegetables under their arms like they'd just found treasure — and I thought: this is what food is supposed to feel like.

Not a transaction. A relationship.

Farmers markets have been growing fast across the United States — from around 2,700 in 1998 to more than 8,600 by 2017, according to USDA's Agricultural Marketing Service. That growth tells a story about what people are hungry for. And I don't just mean produce.

## The Food Is Actually Better

Let me start with the obvious one, because it really is foundational. The food at a good farmers market is better than what you'll find in most grocery stores. Not a little bit better. Noticeably, meaningfully better.

Here's why. When a supermarket tomato gets picked, it's often harvested green or underripe so it can survive a week or more in a supply chain — trucks, distribution centers, cold storage, the store shelf. It gets to you technically red but biologically still unfinished. The flavor compounds that should have developed in those final days on the vine never formed.

A farmers market tomato was probably picked within the last few days, often the day before or the morning of the market. It finished on the plant. The sugars and acids and aromatic compounds that make a tomato taste like a tomato had time to do their work. That's not marketing language. That's the chemistry of ripening.

USDA's Economic Research Service has found that shoppers who buy directly from farmers at markets and roadside stands tend to buy more fruits and vegetables overall, in part because the quality and variety draws them in. You eat more produce when the produce is actually worth eating.

## You're Meeting the People Who Feed You

This one I feel deeply. When you shop at a farmers market, you can ask the person who grew your food exactly how they grew it. You can ask if they use pesticides. You can ask whether the chickens that laid your eggs had access to pasture. You can ask why the garlic this week looks different from last week's.

You cannot do that at a grocery store. At a grocery store, your food is anonymous. At a farmers market, your food has a name and a face and a story.

I've had conversations at my booth that changed the way I think about what I'm growing. People ask questions I hadn't considered. They tell me how they used something I sold them. That feedback loop between grower and eater is part of what makes the whole system healthy. It keeps farmers accountable and keeps eaters informed.

One of my favorite vendors at the markets I attend is Animal Farm, a local outfit that grows vegetables, flowers, and mushrooms. They're doing real things with living soil. Standing at their table feels like getting a short course in regenerative farming. That's what a farmers market can be — an education that tastes good.

## Your Dollars Do More Work Here

When you buy a tomato at a grocery store, a fraction of that dollar makes it back to the farmer. The rest goes to the distributor, the trucking company, the warehouse, the retailer. The farmer's margin in conventional supply chains is often razor thin.

At a farmers market, when you hand money to the person at the booth, that money goes directly to the farm. Sometimes it goes directly into the pocket of the person who planted the seed. That's a pretty different relationship between food dollar and food production.

Research from USDA's Economic Research Service on local food systems shows that direct-to-consumer markets like farmers markets allow farmers to capture a much larger share of the food dollar compared to selling through wholesale or retail channels. That financial return is what allows small farms to stay in business, invest in better practices, and keep growing.

And there's a multiplier effect. An Oregon State University Extension study found that 31 to 92 percent of what customers spend at a farmers market also gets spent at neighboring businesses on the same trip. You're not just supporting the farms. You're supporting the whole local economy around the market.

## It's Where Your Food Community Lives

Farmers markets are one of the last public gathering spaces in American life that are genuinely centered on something real. Not entertainment, not consumption of a product made somewhere else — but food grown nearby, community built around it, people meeting each other over something they all have in common.

I've watched friendships form at market booths. I've seen neighbors figure out they've been buying from the same farmer for years without knowing it. I've watched kids discover that vegetables come from the ground rather than from plastic bags. These things matter. They build the kind of community that takes care of itself.

The Urban Harvest Farmers Market happens every Saturday from 8 to noon at Westheimer and Buffalo Speedway in Houston. There's music. There's hot breakfast. There are farmers who've been showing up every week for years, building relationships with the same customers season after season. That's not a shopping experience. That's a community institution.

## What Happens When We Stop Showing Up

Y'all, I want you to sit with this for a second. Farmers markets only exist if enough people show up. Small farms only stay in business if they can sell their product. Regenerative practices — no-till, cover crops, living soil work — are often more labor-intensive upfront. They require a market that will pay a price that reflects the real cost of growing food well.

When we default to the grocery store because it's cheaper or more convenient, we're not just making a shopping decision. We're voting for a food system. We're saying: the cheapest unit of food, produced any way possible, shipped from anywhere, is what we want.

When we show up at a farmers market, we're voting differently. We're saying that we want to know where our food comes from, that we're willing to pay a fair price for food grown with care, and that we want a food community — not just a food supply.

That vote matters. It adds up. It's one of the most direct forms of influence any of us has over how food gets produced in this country.

Find your local market. Go on a Saturday morning. Bring cash, bring your EBT card, bring your questions. Talk to the farmers. Let your kids squeeze the tomatoes. Come back the next week.

That's how a food community stays alive.

---

## Sources

- [Local Food Systems: Concepts, Impacts, and Issues — USDA Economic Research Service](https://www.ers.usda.gov/webdocs/publications/46393/7054_err97_1_.pdf?v=0)
- [Shopping at Farmers' Markets and Roadside Stands Increases Fruit and Vegetable Demand — USDA ERS](https://www.ers.usda.gov/amber-waves/2018/march/shopping-at-farmers-markets-and-roadside-stands-increases-fruit-and-vegetable-demand)
- [Varied Interests Drive Growing Popularity of Local Foods — USDA ERS](https://www.ers.usda.gov/amber-waves/2010/december/varied-interests-drive-growing-popularity-of-local-foods)
- [Farmers Markets, Other Local Food Marketing Channels Show Strong Growth — USDA ERS](https://ers.usda.gov/data-products/charts-of-note/chart-detail?chartId=78076)
- [Food Assistance Program Connects Low-Income Seniors with Fresh Farm Produce — USDA ERS](https://www.ers.usda.gov/amber-waves/2014/august/food-assistance-program-connects-low-income-seniors-with-fresh-farm-produce)

scottyQuotes: At a grocery store, your food is anonymous. At a farmers market, your food has a name and a face and a story.
When we show up at a farmers market, we're voting differently.
